comments.author: GridGazer
comments.text: Quarter Kevu has 27 points from two races and hasn't won either of them. That's the definition of a champion collecting what's available while the chaos unfolds around him. But let's not pretend VCARB are 'the team everyone else is trying to catch' when Ferrari have won both races and Väisänen has maximum points. This article reads like a PR brief from Faenza.

replies.author: quarter_defender
replies.text: VCARB have produced the last two drivers' champions and Kevu is P2 in the standings despite a puncture in Melbourne and a safety car lottery in China. The Ferrari is fast but it's a one-car team — Cachinho has zero points. Meanwhile Wright is also scoring. The constructors' battle is going to expose Ferrari's weakness, and Kevu will be right there to pick up the pieces when Väisänen eventually has a bad weekend.

replies.author: TifosiForever
replies.text: A 'bad weekend' for Väisänen will come eventually, but he's been flawless so far. Kevu hasn't shown he can beat Väisänen straight up in dry conditions. P2 in China was a well-executed race, but he was 15 seconds behind at the flag. That's not a title fight, that's a title spectator.

comments.author: Kevu2Wright
comments.text: This is the article Kevu deserves. While everyone is obsessing over Cachinho's meltdowns and Roy's pit crew tantrums, the reigning champion is quietly stacking points. 43 points, P2 in the championship, zero mistakes. The VCARB isn't the fastest car on a single lap, but over a race distance it's the most complete package. Suzuka will suit the car's aero efficiency — don't be shocked if Kevu wins on merit.

replies.author: Roy2RB4ever
replies.text: Kevu hasn't won because the VCARB isn't quick enough in qualifying. P4 in China, P2 in Melbourne — good, but not pole positions. Roy had pole in China and would have won without the pit stop disaster. Kevu is a points machine, not a race winner this season.

replies.author: SbinnalaEnjoyer
replies.text: Roy also finished P15 in the sprint and has 27 points from two races. Kevu has 43. A 'points machine' is exactly what wins championships. Ask any former champion whether they'd rather have pole positions or a trophy at the end of the year.

replies.author: TifosiForever
replies.text: You're complaining about qualifying while Väisänen started P8 in both the China sprint and the China race — P8! — and still finished P2 in the sprint and won the Grand Prix. If Kevu needs a front-row start to win, that's a Kevu problem, not a car problem.

comments.author: Zwart_Horse_187
comments.text: Can we address the elephant in the room? Kevu has 43 points, but 7 of those came from fastest lap in Australia while finishing P7. That's a nice bonus, but it's not a performance indicator. In China he was simply the best of the rest behind a dominant Ferrari. Until Kevu actually beats Väisänen wheel-to-wheel or outqualifies him, the championship picture is Väisänen first, daylight second.

replies.author: quarter_defender
replies.text: Väisänen started P8 in China on hards and got lucky with the safety car window. He didn't beat Kevu wheel-to-wheel either — he jumped him on strategy. The Ferrari is fast, but Kevu is maximising every opportunity. That's what champions do.

replies.author: RealityCheck
replies.text: The first true dry qualifying on a normal track will tell us everything. China's sprint qualifying was chaotic with traffic, and the race grid was set separately. Suzuka with no sprint weekend will be the first honest benchmark. If Kevu is within two tenths of pole, he's in the title fight. If he's half a second off, it's a different story.

comments.author: FaktLap1Legend
comments.text: Quarter Kevu is just a budget Sam Fakt with better luck. Fakt has the raw pace to win any race (when he chooses to finish it), while Kevu has to settle for 'consistent points'. The GOAT will awaken in Suzuka. Mark my words.

replies.author: DNF_Fakt
replies.text: Quarter Kevu has two drivers' championships and just finished P2. Sam Fakt has a lap one crash compilation and zero points. The comparison is an insult to Kevu and everyone reading this.

replies.author: ChaosReigns
replies.text: This account is performance art at this point and I'm kind of here for it. Keep fighting the good fight, FaktLap1Legend. The 2026 season needs a court jester.

comments.author: SpringfieldStan
comments.text: Interesting that an article about Kevu completely ignores Sarah Springfield, who led the Chinese GP on merit before her spin and still finished P7. She has 14 points from two races in an Aston Martin that wasn't supposed to be quick. If we're talking about drivers maximising results, Springfield deserves more ink than Kevu's 'consistent points'.

replies.author: GridGazer
replies.text: Springfield's spin from the lead is a major "what if." If she'd held on and won, we'd be talking about a three-way title fight. Instead she's P6 in the championship and the Aston Martin story is still an undercard. She's been the most underrated driver on the grid for two years running.

comments.author: CachinhoPressure
comments.text: Reading about Kevu's 'title conversation' while knowing Cachinho has zero points and six penalty points is painful. VCARB have two drivers scoring. Ferrari have one. If the constructors' championship swings on that, Kevu might end up with another trophy not because he was faster, but because Ferrari couldn't field a complete team.

replies.author: TifosiForever
replies.text: This is exactly the worry. Väisänen is doing everything right, but VCARB's two-car consistency will erode the constructors' lead if Cachinho doesn't start scoring. Kevu doesn't need to beat Väisänen — he just needs to wait for Ferrari's second seat to become an anchor.
